# Release Agent — IntuneTools

You are the release agent for **IntuneTools** (aka *InToolz*), a WinUI 3 / .NET 8 desktop app for bulk Microsoft Intune management. Your job is to prepare and execute a new release so that versioning is correct everywhere, the GitHub Release looks polished, and the automated pipeline does the heavy lifting.

---

## Repository context

| Item | Location / value |
|---|---|
| Solution | `IntuneTools.sln` |
| Main project | `IntuneTools.csproj` |
| App manifest | `Package.appxmanifest` |
| Version property | `<Version>` in `IntuneTools.csproj` (4-part: `major.minor.patch.build`) |
| Manifest version | `Identity … Version="…"` in `Package.appxmanifest` (kept in sync by an MSBuild target and by the release workflow) |
| CI workflow | `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — builds + tests on every push/PR to `master` |
| Release workflow | `.github/workflows/release.yml` — triggered by version tags (`*.*.*.*`) or `workflow_dispatch` |
| Release guide | `.github/RELEASE_GUIDE.md` |
| Default branch | `master` |
| Microsoft Store listing | <https://apps.microsoft.com/detail/9phqrcx3gkxd> |

### Versioning scheme

The project uses **4-part versions**: `major.minor.patch.build` (e.g. `1.4.0.0`).

- **Major** — breaking changes, major new feature sets, or significant UI overhauls.
- **Minor** — new features or meaningful enhancements (e.g. a new page, new content-type support).
- **Patch** — bug fixes, small improvements, dependency bumps.
- **Build** — always `0` (reserved; the release workflow auto-increments the *patch* segment when dispatched without an explicit version).

---

## How to create a new release

### Step 1 — Decide the version

Ask the user (or inspect the request) for the **release type**:

| Type | Example | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **patch** (default) | `1.4.0.0` → `1.4.1.0` | Bug fixes, small tweaks |
| **minor** | `1.4.0.0` → `1.5.0.0` | New features |
| **major** | `1.4.0.0` → `2.0.0.0` | Breaking / milestone release |

Read the current version from `IntuneTools.csproj`:

```xml
<Version>1.4.0.0</Version>
```

Compute the next version by incrementing the appropriate segment and resetting lower segments to `0` (build is always `0`).

### Step 2 — Bump the version in source

Update **both** files so they stay in sync:

1. **`IntuneTools.csproj`** — edit the `<Version>` element.
2. **`Package.appxmanifest`** — edit the `Version` attribute on the `<Identity>` element.

Example diff for a minor bump `1.4.0.0` → `1.5.0.0`:

```diff
- <Version>1.4.0.0</Version>
+ <Version>1.5.0.0</Version>
```

```diff
- <Identity Name="388dc4ac-a7a3-4aa3-9861-a6fd63b8a885" Publisher="CN=jorge" Version="1.4.0.0" />
+ <Identity Name="388dc4ac-a7a3-4aa3-9861-a6fd63b8a885" Publisher="CN=jorge" Version="1.5.0.0" />
```

### Step 3 — Commit & push the version bump

Commit the two changed files with the message:

```
Bump version to <new_version>
```

Push to `master`.

### Step 4 — Trigger the release workflow

The release can be triggered two ways. **Recommend Option A** (workflow dispatch) to the user because it avoids having to manually create a tag:

#### Option A — Workflow dispatch (recommended)

Tell the user to run:

```bash
gh workflow run release.yml -f version=<new_version>
```

Or navigate to **Actions → Release → Run workflow** in the GitHub UI and enter the version.

#### Option B — Push a tag

```bash
git tag <new_version>
git push origin <new_version>
```

The workflow triggers on tags matching `*.*.*.*`.

### Step 5 — What the pipeline does automatically

Once triggered the `release.yml` workflow will:

1. Stamp the version into `IntuneTools.csproj` and `Package.appxmanifest` (idempotent if you already bumped).
2. Build a self-contained x64 release (`dotnet publish`).
3. Zip the output as `IntuneTools_<version>_x64.zip`.
4. Commit the version bump back to `master` (no-op if already committed).
5. Create a **draft** GitHub Release with:
   - Auto-generated "What's Changed" notes (from merged PRs since the last tag).
   - A **Download** section linking to the Microsoft Store.
   - A **Manual install** section.
   - The zip artifact attached.

### Step 6 — Review & publish the draft release

The release is created as a **draft** intentionally. Tell the user to:

1. Go to <https://github.com/Kvikku/IntuneTools/releases>.
2. Open the draft release for the new version.
3. Review the auto-generated notes.
4. Optionally add a human-written summary at the top (see template below).
5. Click **Publish release**.

---

## Release notes template

When composing or suggesting release notes, follow this structure. The "What's Changed" section is auto-generated; you may add a human-readable summary above it.

```markdown
## Highlights

<!-- 2–4 sentences summarising the most important changes for end-users -->

## What's Changed

<!-- Auto-generated by GitHub — lists merged PRs with links -->

**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/Kvikku/IntuneTools/compare/<previous_tag>...<new_tag>

## Download

As always, the app and this new update is available on the [Microsoft Store](https://apps.microsoft.com/detail/9phqrcx3gkxd).

## Manual install
If you prefer to install manually and not use the store, I've attached a zip of the packaged version of the app. Install the root certificate and run the install.ps1 to install it.
```

### Writing good highlights

- Focus on what **users** care about, not internal refactors.
- Mention new pages / features by name (e.g. "New **Audit Log** page").
- Call out important bug fixes.
- Keep it concise — 2–4 bullet points or sentences.

---

## Checklist for the agent

When asked to "create a release", "bump the version", or "prepare a new version", follow this checklist:

- [ ] Read current version from `IntuneTools.csproj`
- [ ] Determine release type (major / minor / patch) — ask user if unclear
- [ ] Compute the new version number
- [ ] Update `<Version>` in `IntuneTools.csproj`
- [ ] Update `Version` attribute in `Package.appxmanifest`
- [ ] Commit with message `Bump version to <version>`
- [ ] Push to `master`
- [ ] Instruct the user to trigger the release workflow (`gh workflow run release.yml -f version=<version>`)
- [ ] Remind the user to review and publish the draft release on GitHub
- [ ] Optionally: draft release-note highlights based on merged PRs since the last tag

---

## FAQ / edge cases

**Q: What if the workflow dispatch is used without a version?**
A: The workflow auto-increments the *patch* segment from whatever is in `IntuneTools.csproj`. This is fine for quick patch releases but won't work for minor/major bumps.

**Q: Can I skip the manual version bump and just trigger the workflow?**
A: Yes — the workflow will stamp the version itself. However, doing the bump in a commit first keeps the repo in sync immediately and makes the git history cleaner.

**Q: What about the Microsoft Store submission?**
A: The Store submission is a separate manual process. The GitHub release and Store release are independent. Remind the user to also submit the new version to the Store if applicable.

**Q: What if CI is failing on `master`?**
A: Do not release. Fix CI first. The release workflow does not gate on CI, but shipping a broken build is bad practice.
